input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100, featuring a first vertical robot axis, a permanent magnet synchronous motor for precise motion control, and a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for real-time processing and coordination of robotic operations in industrial manufacturing environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real-time visualization services via an interactive dashboard for operational monitoring, along with advanced friction model identification capabilities (both white-box and black-box) to enhance predictive maintenance and system optimization for the physical robot.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) employs SysML for multi-level abstraction modeling (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT address system complexity. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define component & connector perspectives, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is handled via MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines (v1.7), integrated with MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and code in C++ or Java for detailed implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for geometric structural modeling via Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs), behavioral modeling using state machines (SysML v1.7) and MontiArc Automaton, and analytical simulations via MathWorks Simulink. Data sources consist of contextual physical system traces aggregated for specific purposes, managed through integration of heterogeneous modeling tools (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and languages across requirements, functional, logical, and technical abstraction levels. Model relationships are defined by component & connector perspectives, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition, while integrating stakeholder perspectives for comprehensive system representation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical acting components include a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis with integrated gear transmissions, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC system that manages robot axes through precise motion control and real-time feedback.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises two position encoders per drive, with the primary encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. All sensor data is collected as time-series streams and transmitted via MQTT protocol for real-time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated through comparison of simulated data versus measured data to quantify deviations, with sensor arrangements reducing backlash and positional uncertainty. Continuous improvement of both white-box and black-box friction models enhances accuracy and minimizes prediction errors.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Unidirectional data flow from physical to digital using MQTT protocol for streaming time series sensor data, as specified by 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT' and the digital shadow's definition of 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for transmitting time-series sensor data. Network topology, bandwidth requirements, latency, reliability mechanisms, redundancy strategies, and security protocols are not specified in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a structured methodology comprising four key steps: (1) problem identification to define objectives and scope, (2) asset and model analysis to determine relevant physical components and their digital representations, (3) use or creation of calculations or models for simulation and analysis, and (4) data identification to specify required inputs and outputs for real-time synchronization between physical and digital entities.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates actionable insights through physical relationship analysis, such as friction loss derivation and understanding of physical relationships, to improve robot control accuracy. Decision-making is supported via real-time analytics and user interaction through dashboard visualizations for monitoring and intervention.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) standard for digital twin interoperability and the SysML modeling language for systems engineering, ensuring adherence to industry specifications for data exchange and model integration.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately and specifically describes the physical system, including the robot model (MABI MAX 100), the specific axis, motor type, and controller, as detailed in the use case section. Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100', 'first vertical robot axis at the base is used', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

Correctly identifies the visualization dashboard and the specific capability of identifying friction models using both white-box and black-box approaches. However, the mention of 'predictive maintenance' is not explicitly supported by the text, which focuses on 'improve robot motion accuracy'. Supported by quotes: 'visualization in a dashboard', 'identification of joint friction, using both white-box and black-box models', 'improve robot motion accuracy'.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurately reflects the 'Modeling Software and Systems' section of the source text. It correctly lists tools and methodologies such as 'SysML', 'RFLP', 'ACME', 'AADL', 'MontiArc', and 'MathWorks Simulink', describing their roles in abstraction, architecture definition, and behavior modeling.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time' data and 'time series' but does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs between physical and digital components as requested by the definition. Quote: 'Our approach leverages digital shadows — real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior'.

The source text describes the creation of a single digital shadow for the first axis of a specific robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between multiple twins. Quote: 'The class FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow.'

The paper describes the internal structure and data flow of a Digital Shadow (a passive entity), but does not describe the overall orchestration, resource management, or system-wide coordination mechanisms of a broader Digital Twin system. Quote: 'In practical terms, this means that a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.'

While the document mentions Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT, it focuses on the internal integration of the robot's data into a shadow (vertical integration) rather than describing integration patterns with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins. Quote: 'In this paper, we apply and refine a method to construct digital shadows that integrate structural and behavioral models, with operational data...'

The text explicitly states that 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and describes 'Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)' as a component & connector perspective. The definition of data sources as 'a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose' is directly quoted and integrated. The integration of heterogeneous tools like 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc' and different abstraction levels is also supported by the text.

The extraction accurately describes the actuator and control system. It correctly identifies the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor' ('The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor') and the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' control system ('The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'). While it mentions 'integrated gear transmissions', it lacks the specific details about the gearwheel and cycloidal gearbox found in the source, slightly reducing the depth.

The description is correct and specific to the text. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox'). It accurately notes that the sensor arrangement reduces 'backlash and positional uncertainty' ('This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional uncertainty'). It also correctly mentions the continuous improvement of 'white-box and black-box friction models' ('Existing research falls short in providing methods for continuously improving both white- and black-box friction models').

block_5_judge.reasoning: The source documents do not contain information describing the distinct lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin (e.g., development, operation, maintenance) nor do they discuss transition management between phases or lifecycle governance. While the text outlines a creation methodology (which relates to the twinning process), it does not define the lifecycle stages or representation types at different phases as defined by the characteristic.

The extraction accurately summarizes the four-step methodology described in Section II.C ('How to create Digital Shadows') and applied in Section IV.A. The text lists: '1) Problem identification... 2) Asset and model analysis... 3) Use or creation of calculations or models... 4) Data identification'. The extraction reflects these steps correctly.

The extraction identifies the goal to 'derive the friction... within the gearbox' to 'improve the robot control’s accuracy' and understand 'physical relationships'. This is supported by quotes: 'derive the friction (static, kinetic, and viscous) within the gearbox of the robot’s first axis' and 'improve the robot control’s accuracy'. It also mentions dashboard visualization, which matches 'visualization in a dashboard'. However, the phrase 'real-time analytics' is not explicitly found in the text, though 'real-time' is associated with the shadow. 'Decision-making' is framed as improving control accuracy rather than a broader decision support process.

The extraction correctly identifies compliance with the 'Asset Administration Shell (AAS)' standard and the 'SysML' modeling language. The text explicitly states in the Abstract: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and in the Index Terms: 'SysML'. Section II.A also mentions 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML'.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents do not mention data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, or consent management. The text is entirely focused on the technical modeling of robot friction and digital shadow implementation.

The documents do not discuss cybersecurity protocols, access control, or digital threat mitigation. While 'The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range' is mentioned, this refers to a physical operational constraint, not a digital security or safety protocol as defined in the characteristic description.
